target.name: Ephos
target.founder: Andrea Rocchetto, Francesco Ceccarelli, Ciro Pentangelo

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Chips & Semiconductors (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 10 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Ephos is a seed-stage semiconductor company founded by Andrea Rocchetto, Francesco Ceccarelli, and Ciro Pentangelo. The company has raised €41.5M from EU sources plus $8.5M in seed capital. The valuation universe consists of 11 total comparables: 3 public peers (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private peers operating in Chips & Semiconductors, including Cerebras, SambaNova Systems, PsiQuantum, Etched, and Tenstorrent.

The peer-median method is not applicable in this analysis. While 10 same-sector peers exist in the Chips & Semiconductors universe, none satisfy the narrow filtering criteria required for reliable triangulation. Specifically, the search yielded 0 same-stage comparables, 1 adjacent-stage peer, and 0 similar-valuation-tier datapoints—all below the minimum threshold of 3 required to establish a defensible median. Applying an all-sector median would introduce unacceptable noise given the capital intensity and stage-specific dynamics of semiconductor ventures.

Without a sufficiently sized peer-median cohort, this analysis cannot generate a triangulated valuation range from the data provided. Additional comparable transactions at the seed or early-growth stage within focused hardware or semiconductor subsectors would be required to anchor the valuation. Key uncertainties include the absence of stage-appropriate precedents and the heterogeneity of the private peer set by business model and capital requirements. Further diligence on technology differentiation and addressable market would inform valuation setting.
